title: Better Chroropleths with DataShine
description:
This post is inspired by Oliver O'Brien & James Cheshire (2016) DataShine 
application which overlays urban areas onto Choropleths so the data
shines through the map. Thereby overcomming a common shortfall with Choropleths
on areas with uneven population densities.
contents:
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, results = "as-is")
library(magrittr)
library(ggmap)



Objective
This post is inspired by Oliver O’Brien & James Cheshire (2016) DataShine application which overlays urban areas onto Choropleths so that the data shines through the urban areas on the amp. This overcomes a common criticism of Choropleth maps that they make data in less densely populated areas appear more prominent that it is.
Data
Data are sourced from the UK Data Service:
https://borders.ukdataservice.ac.uk/bds.html
http://infuse2011gf.ukdataservice.ac.uk/infusewizgeo.aspx
https://borders.ukdataservice.ac.uk/easy_download.html
And the Ordnance Survey Boundary-Line and Straegi datasets.
https://osdatahub.os.uk/downloads/open
Load the Shapefiles


# Load Output Areas By Country
engoa <- sf::read_sf("~/data/England_oac_2011/england_oac_2011.shp")
scooa <- sf::read_sf("~/data/Scotland_oac_2011/scotland_oac_2011.shp")
niroa <- sf::read_sf("~/data/NIreland_sa_2011_oac/nireland_sa_2011_oac.shp")
waloa <- sf::read_sf("~/data/Wales_oac_2011/wales_oac_2011.shp")



Load the Year Left Work Data


yearlw <- readr::read_csv("Data_AGE_UNIT_YEARLW.csv") %>%
  dplyr::select(-CDU_ID, -X18) %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(GEO_CODE))

yearlw[,5:16] <- yearlw[,5:16] %>% purrr::map_if(is.character, as.numeric) # Convert to Numeric



Join Together


# Join Together
uk_yearlw <- 
  engoa %>% dplyr::select(GEO_CODE = code) %>%
  dplyr::bind_rows(scooa %>% dplyr::select(GEO_CODE = code)) %>%
  dplyr::bind_rows(niroa %>% dplyr::select(GEO_CODE = code)) %>%
  dplyr::bind_rows(waloa %>% dplyr::select(GEO_CODE = code)) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(yearlw) 



Create Bounding Box of 40km Around Carlisle
We explore Carlisle to test the data at the border.


# Get Lon-Lat Coordinate and Create Bounding Box of n meters
lonlat <- ggmap::geocode("Carlisle, Cumbria")
lonlat_wgs84 <-  lonlat %>% as.matrix() %>% sf::st_point() %>%
  sf::st_sfc() %>% sf::st_set_crs(4326)
lonlat_bng <- sf::st_transform(lonlat_wgs84, 27700)
buffer_40k <- sf::st_buffer(lonlat_bng, 40e3)

bbox <- sf::st_as_sfc(sf::st_bbox(buffer_40k))
oa_bb <- uk_yearlw %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)



Load the Ordnance Survey Data Clipped to Bounding Box


admin_line <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/admin_line.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
coast_line <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/coastline.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
foreshore_region <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/foreshor_region.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
urban <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/urban_region.shp")
a_road <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/a_road.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
motorway <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/motorway.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
lake <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/lakes_region.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
river <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/rivers_line.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
woodland <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/woodland_region.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
national_park <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/national_park.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
general_text <- sf::read_sf("~/data/strtgi_essh_gb/data/general_text.shp") %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)



Process Data for Plotting


# Load UK
uk <- sf::read_sf("~/data/bdline_essh_gb/Data/Supplementary_Country/country_region.shp")
uk <- sf::st_union(uk) # Dissolve Polygons into single region for the UK
uk_bb <- uk %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
uk_diff <- sf::st_difference(bbox, uk_bb)

urban_bb <- urban %>% sf::st_intersection(bbox)
urban_bb_union <- sf::st_union(urban_bb)
urban_diff <- sf::st_difference(bbox, urban_bb_union) # Create hole in bounding box

national_park_diff <- sf::st_difference(national_park, urban_bb_union) # Create hole

main_river <- dplyr::filter(river, grepl("main", LEGEND, ignore.case = TRUE))

oa_bb <- oa_bb %>% dplyr::mutate(employment_rate = F677/F676) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(location_quotient = employment_rate/mean(employment_rate))

national_boundary <- admin_line %>% dplyr::filter(CODE == 5710)



Plot Map


calculate_labels <- function(x, n = 4){
  o <- seq(min(x), max(x), (max(x)-min(x))/n)
  paste0(round(100*o,0),"%")
}

ggplot2::ggplot() +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = oa_bb, ggplot2::aes(fill = location_quotient), colour = NA) + 
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = urban_diff,  fill="lightgrey", colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = uk_diff, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5, colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = national_boundary, linetype = 2) + 
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = coast_line, colour = NA) +
 # ggplot2::geom_sf(data = foreshore_region, colour = "steelblue", fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = national_park_diff, colour = NA, fill = "darkgreen", alpha = 0.25)   +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = lake, colour = NA, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = main_river, colour = "steelblue", fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = a_road, colour = "darkgrey") +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = motorway, colour = "darkgrey", lwd = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf_text(data = general_text %>%
                          dplyr::filter(FONT_SIZE>=7) %>%
                          dplyr::filter(nchar(NAME)>1),
                        ggplot2::aes(label = NAME, size = FONT_SIZE),show.legend = FALSE) +
  ggplot2::scale_size_continuous(range = c(2,3)) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkorange", high = "white",
                               labels = calculate_labels(oa_bb$employment_rate),
                               name = "Employment") +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
  ggplot2::theme(axis.text = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 axis.line = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 axis.ticks = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank()
                 ) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "", y = "") 




Roll Up Data to MSOA and Plot


# Load Lookup
lookup <- readr::read_csv("all-areas-lookup.csv")

oa_bb <- oa_bb %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    lookup %>%
      dplyr::select(GEO_CODE = OASA_GSS_ID, LSOADZ_GSS_ID, MSOAIZ_GSS_ID) 
  )

msoa <- oa_bb %>%
  dplyr::group_by(MSOAIZ_GSS_ID) %>%
  dplyr::summarise(F677 = sum(F677), F676 = sum(F676)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(employment_rate = F677/F676) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(location_quotient = employment_rate / mean(employment_rate))

ggplot2::ggplot() +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = msoa, ggplot2::aes(fill = location_quotient), colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = urban_diff,  fill="lightgrey", colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = uk_diff, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5, colour = NA) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = national_boundary, linetype = 2) + 
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = coast_line, colour = NA) +
  # ggplot2::geom_sf(data = foreshore_region, colour = "steelblue", fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = national_park_diff, colour = NA, fill = "darkgreen", alpha = 0.25)   +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = lake, colour = NA, fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = main_river, colour = "steelblue", fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.5) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = a_road, colour = "darkgrey") +
  ggplot2::geom_sf(data = motorway, colour = "darkgrey", lwd = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_sf_text(data = general_text %>%
                          dplyr::filter(FONT_SIZE>=7) %>%
                          dplyr::filter(nchar(NAME)>1),
                        ggplot2::aes(label = NAME, size = FONT_SIZE),show.legend = FALSE) +
  ggplot2::scale_size_continuous(range = c(2,3)) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_gradient(low = "darkorange", high = "white",
                               labels = calculate_labels(msoa$employment_rate),
                               name = "Employment") +
  ggplot2::theme_minimal() +
  ggplot2::theme(axis.text = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 axis.line = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 axis.ticks = ggplot2::element_blank(),
                 panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank()
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(x = "", y = "")

title: Comparison of Sentiment Analysis over Time
description: Comparison of PAC Hearing Oral Evidence.

title: Sentiment Analysis of Public Accounts Committee Oral Evidence Hearing
description:
Exploratory analysis to understand the utility of sentiment analysis in 
evaluating qualiative information.
contents:
Objective
To explore the utility of sentiment analysis in evaluating qualitative information.
Fetch and Preprocess Data
I extract a House of Commons Public Accounts Committee Oral Evidence Hearing for the Inquiry into Defence Capabilities and the Equipment Plan (2019-2029) and tidy the data so that the dialogue is matched to the speaker and the question.


document <- httr::GET("https://committees.parliament.uk/oralevidence/429/html/") %>%
  httr::content() %>% 
  xml2::xml_find_all("//p") %>%
  xml2::xml_text()

meta <- document[1:19] # Can this be detected automatically?
text <- document[20:length(document)]

witnesses <- meta[18] %>%
  stringr::str_split(", | and |: |\\.") %>%
  unlist()

witnesses <- witnesses[2:6]
witnesses <- paste0(witnesses,":")
witnesses <- paste(witnesses, collapse = "|")

members <- meta[7:9] %>% 
  stringr::str_split(", | and |: |\\.|and |; ") %>%
  unlist() %>%
  stringr::str_replace(" \\(Chair\\)", "")

stop_words <- c("", "Members present", "NAO Director", 
                "Director of Parliamentary Relations", 
                "Alternate Treasury Officer of Accounts", 
                "were in attendance", "HM Treasury", "NAO",
                "Comptroller & Auditor General",
                "Liaison Committee member also present")

members <- members[!members %in% stop_words]
members <- paste0(members, ":")
members <- paste(members, collapse = "|")

people <- paste(members, witnesses,"Chair:", sep = "|")
questions <- "^Q[0-9]{3}|^Q[0-9]{2}|^Q[0-9]{1}"

tidy_text <- tibble::tibble(text) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(question = stringr::str_extract(text, questions)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(person = stringr::str_extract(text, people)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(person = stringr::str_sub(person, 1, nchar(person)-1)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dialogue = stringr::str_remove(text, people)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dialogue = stringr::str_remove(dialogue, questions)) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(dialogue = stringr::str_trim(dialogue)) %>%
  tidyr::fill(question, person, .direction = "down") %>%
  dplyr::select(question, person, dialogue) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(question, person) %>%
  tidyr::replace_na(list(question = "Opening"))

committee <- meta[2]
oral <- meta[3]
date <- meta[4]

meta_df <- tibble::tibble(committee, oral, date, members, witnesses)

tidy_document <- list(meta_df, body = tidy_text)
tidy_document


[[1]]
# A tibble: 1 x 5
  committee    oral           date    members         witnesses       
  <chr>        <chr>          <chr>   <chr>           <chr>           
1 Public Acco~ Oral evidence~ Thursd~ Meg Hillier:|G~ Sir Simon Bollo~

$body
# A tibble: 268 x 3
# Groups:   question, person [186]
   question person          dialogue                                  
   <chr>    <chr>           <chr>                                     
 1 Opening  Chair           Welcome to the Public Accounts Committee ~
 2 Opening  Chair           I would like to introduce our witnesses. ~
 3 Opening  Chair           We have a big job to get through today, i~
 4 Opening  Sir Stephen Lo~ I wish it had been quicker, candidly. It ~
 5 Opening  Sir Stephen Lo~ I think we are very, very close to the en~
 6 Q1       Chair           It is a real disappointment for something~
 7 Q1       Sir Stephen Lo~ Yes, of course. We have already made a nu~
 8 Q2       Chair           I would hope so, because an individual ma~
 9 Q2       Chair           Let me move on to the general issues rela~
10 Q2       Sir Stephen Lo~ It is certainly not to hide anything. Whe~
# ... with 258 more rows

Sentiment Analysis
I use Tyler Rinker’s excellent sentimentr package to calculate text polarity sentiment at the sentence level. This attempts to take into account valence shifters (i.e., negators, amplifiers (intensifiers), de-amplifiers (downtoners), and adversative conjunctions) while maintaining speed. By default sentimentr uses the Jockers 2017 dictionary.
Sentiment by Person
The plot below shows the average sentiment by person and the variation in sentiment. Gareth Davies said the least, but was positive. The chair (Meg Hillier) had the most variation.


tidy_document$body %>%
  dplyr::mutate(sentence = sentimentr::get_sentences(dialogue)) %$%
  sentimentr::sentiment_by(dialogue, list(person)) %>%
  plot()




Sentiment by Question
The plot below shows the distribution of sentiment by question. The most positive question and response was Q71. The least positive was Q6. This is most useful in understanding the difficult questions the MOD faces.


tidy_document$body %>%
  dplyr::mutate(sentence = sentimentr::get_sentences(dialogue)) %$%
  sentimentr::sentiment_by(dialogue, list(question)) %>%
  plot()




Emotional Valence Over Time
The plot below shows emotional valence over time. The hearing starts with the most difficult questions, before getting position


tidy_document$body %>%
  dplyr::mutate(sentence = sentimentr::get_sentences(dialogue)) %$%
  sentimentr::sentiment_by(dialogue, list(person)) %>%
  sentimentr::uncombine() %>%
  plot()




Highlighter Test
The sentimentr package contains a highlighter function that produces an HTML document with positive sentences highlighted green and negative sentences highlighted red. This is a useful tool to check the analysis.


highlight_test <- tidy_document$body %>%
  dplyr::mutate(sentence = sentimentr::get_sentences(dialogue)) %$%
  sentimentr::sentiment_by(dialogue, list(question, person)) 

attr(highlight_test, "averaging.function") <- sentimentr::average_downweighted_zero
sentimentr::highlight(highlight_test,)



Conclusion
Sentiment analysis has merit when analysing large volumes of data. It enable the user to see the variation in sentiment on aggregate, in groups and over time. This can help inform where to pay attention to specific elements of text.

title: Cluster Points based on Distance
description: A recipe to cluster coordinates based on a specified distance between them.
contents:
Load margrittr. Functions from other packages are called directly with ::.


library(magrittr)



Generate function to calculate new coordinates given current coordinates an angle in degrees (a) and a given distance (d).


#' Convert degrees to radians
deg2rad  <- function(x) {x * pi/180} 

#' Calculate new coordinates for point given angle and distance
distance_from_point <- function(coord, a, d){
  x <- coord[1]
  y <- coord[2]
  
  xx <- x + (d * cos(deg2rad(a)))
  yy <- y + (d * sin(deg2rad(a)))
  
  c(x = xx, y = yy)
} 



Generate points with two distinct clusters and plot.


label <- c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G")
nodes <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  distance_from_point(c(0,0), 0, 0),
  distance_from_point(c(0,0), 60, -10),
  distance_from_point(c(0,0), 120, -10),
  distance_from_point(c(0,0), 120, -20),
  distance_from_point(c(0,0), 120, -30),
  distance_from_point(c(40,0), 0, 0),
  distance_from_point(c(40,0), 90, -10)
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(id = label) %>%
  dplyr::select(.data$id, .data$x, .data$y)


ggplot2::ggplot(nodes, ggplot2::aes(x, y, label = id)) + 
  ggplot2::geom_point() +
  ggplot2::geom_label()




Calculate the distance between each point and plot.


links <- tidyr::expand_grid(from = label, to = label) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    nodes %>% 
      dplyr::rename(from = .data$id, 
                    from_x = .data$x, 
                    from_y = .data$y),
    by = "from"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::left_join(
    nodes %>% 
      dplyr::rename(to = .data$id, 
                    to_x = .data$x, 
                    to_y = .data$y),
    by = "to"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(distance = sqrt((.data$to_x - .data$from_x)^2 + 
                                  (.data$to_y - .data$from_y)^2)) 

links10m <- links %>% dplyr::filter(.data$distance <= 10)
ggplot2::ggplot(links10m) + 
  ggplot2::geom_segment(ggplot2::aes(x = from_x, y = from_y, 
                                     xend = to_x, yend = to_y)) + 
  ggplot2::geom_label(data = nodes, ggplot2::aes(x = x, y = y, label = label))




Loop through the unique labels and list the points that are within a specified distance (10 units).


groups <- label %>% 
  purrr::map(function(x){
   links10m %>% 
      dplyr::filter(.data$from == x) %>% dplyr::pull(.data$to)
  })

pander::pander(groups)


A, B and C
A, B and C
A, B, C and D
C, D and E
D and E
F and G
F and G

For each of the groups check if any of the points in the group are within any of the other groups. If they are then concatinate the vector with union. Filter the unique variables


clusters <- groups %>%
  purrr::map(function(x){
    purrr::map(groups, function(y) if(any(unlist(x) %in% unlist(y))) union(x,y)) %>%
      unlist() %>% unique() %>% stringr::str_sort()
  }) %>%
  unique()

pander::pander(clusters)


A, B, C, D and E
F and G

Alternative Method 1
Create a graph and perform a depth first search


g <- igraph::graph_from_data_frame(links10m, directed = TRUE, vertices = nodes)
plot(g)



purrr::map(label, function(x){ 
  ord <- igraph::dfs(g, x, unreachable = FALSE)$order %>% as.numeric()
  ord <- ord[!is.na(ord)]
  label[ord] %>% stringr::str_sort()
}) %>%
  unique()


[[1]]
[1] "A" "B" "C" "D" "E"

[[2]]
[1] "F" "G"

Alternative Method 2
Draw a circle with radius equal to distance / 2 and merge overlaping and adjacent shapes.
Benchmarking


system.time({
  label %>% 
  purrr::map(function(x){
   links10m %>% 
      dplyr::filter(.data$from == x) %>% dplyr::pull(.data$to)
  }) %>%
    purrr::map(function(x){
    purrr::map(groups, function(y) if(any(unlist(x) %in% unlist(y))) union(x,y)) %>%
      unlist() %>% unique() %>% stringr::str_sort()
  }) %>%
  unique()
})


   user  system elapsed 
   0.02    0.00    0.02 

system.time({
  g <- igraph::graph_from_data_frame(links10m, directed = TRUE, vertices = nodes)
  purrr::map(label, function(x){ 
    ord <- igraph::dfs(g, x, unreachable = FALSE)$order %>% as.numeric()
    ord <- ord[!is.na(ord)]
    label[ord] %>% stringr::str_sort()
  }) %>%
  unique()
})


   user  system elapsed 
   0.01    0.00    0.01

title: Recipe to Create Tilegrams
description:
A recipe to create a Tilegram. This is where landmasses are converted to the
same size polygon.
contents:
This blog post shows how to create tilegrams using simple triganometry and the optimisation algorithm in the clue package. Load required packages p <- c("sp", "rgeos","rworldmap","ggplot2", "plotly", "plyr", "clue") # Load required packages sapply(p, function(x){if(!require(x,char=T)) install.packages(x); require(x,char=T)}); rm(p) sp rgeos rworldmap ggplot2 plotly plyr clue TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE Load a map of Africa from the rworldmap package ## Get Africa Data data("countriesCoarseLessIslands") # Load simple map without islands afr <- countriesCoarseLessIslands[which(!is.na(countriesCoarseLessIslands@data$REGION) & # Subset for Africa countriesCoarseLessIslands@data$REGION=="Africa"),] ## Project the map to use an equidistance grid wgs84 <- '+proj=longlat +datum=WGS84' wrldequi <- "+init=EPSG:32663" afr <- spTransform(afr, CRS(wrldequi)) Create helper functions # Function to convert degrees to radians (trigonemetry) deg2rad <- function(deg) {(deg * pi) / (180)} # Function to calculate hexagon side length from a given area hex_side <- function(area) (3^0.25)*sqrt(2*(area/9)) # Function to calculate the area of a hexagon given it size hex_area <- function(side) ((3*sqrt(3))/2*side) # Function to draw a hexagon draw_hex <- function(area=hex_area(1), offset_x = 0, offset_y = 0, id=1, tessellate=F){ side_length <- hex_side(area) A <- sin(deg2rad(30)) * side_length B <- sin(deg2rad(60)) * side_length C <- side_length (x <- c(0, 0, B, 2*B, 2*B, B) + (offset_x*B*2) + ifelse(tessellate == T, B, 0)) (y <- c(A+C, A, 0, A, A+C, 2*C) + (offset_y*(A+C))) Polygons(list(Polygon(coords = matrix(c(x,y),ncol=2),hole = F)),ID=id) } # Function to get area of polygons getArea <- function(x) { getAreaPolygons = function(x) { holes = unlist(lapply(x@Polygons, function(x) x@hole)) areas = unlist(lapply(x@Polygons, function(x) x@area)) area = ifelse(holes, -1, 1) * areas area } sum(unlist(lapply(x@polygons, getAreaPolygons))) } # Function to get the average area of polygons getAvgArea <- function(x){ l <- length(x) avgArea <- getArea(x)/l return(avgArea) } # Function to draw hexagon tiles in a tessellated grid draw_hexTiles <- function(area, offset_x_start=0, offset_x_end=4, offset_y_start=0, offset_y_end =4, id){ grid <- expand.grid(offset_x_start:offset_x_end, offset_y_start:offset_y_end) grid$tessellate <- grid[,2] %% 2 == 0 hexes <- SpatialPolygons(lapply(1:nrow(grid), function(i){ draw_hex(area, offset_x = grid[i,1], offset_y = grid[i,2], id =i, tessellate = grid[i,3]) })) names(grid) <- c("offset_x", "offset_y", "tessellate") grid <- data.frame(id = 1:nrow(grid),grid) SpatialPolygonsDataFrame(hexes, grid) } # Function to create tessellated hexagon tiles over a bounding box hex_tiles <- function(x, cellsize=NULL){ if(is.null(cellsize)) cellsize <- getAvgArea(x) b <- bbox(x) dx <- b["x", "max"] - b["x", "min"] dy <- b["y", "max"] - b["y", "min"] C <- hex_side(cellsize) A <- sin(deg2rad(30)) * C B <- sin(deg2rad(60)) * C hexAcross <- ceiling(dx/(B*2)) hexUp <- ceiling(dy/((A+C))) offset_x_start <- floor(b["x", "min"]/(B*2)) offset_y_start <- floor(b["y", "min"]/((A+C))) offset_x_end <- offset_x_start + hexAcross offset_y_end <- offset_y_start + hexUp hex_grid <- draw_hexTiles(cellsize, offset_x_start, offset_x_end, offset_y_start, offset_y_end, i) proj4string(hex_grid) <- proj4string(x) return(hex_grid) } The function below creates a tilegram in R by implementing the ‘Hungarian Algorithm’ in the clue package makeTilegram <- function(sp,cellsize=NULL){ tiles <- hex_tiles(sp,cellsize) # Create hexagon tiles # sp_b <- rgeos::gBuffer(afr, width = 1.1*hex_side(getAvgArea(sp))) # tiles <- tiles[sp_b,] pts <- rgeos::gCentroid(sp,byid = T) pts <- SpatialPointsDataFrame(pts, data.frame(pt_id = row.names(pts), stringsAsFactors = F)) tileCentroids <- rgeos::gCentroid(tiles, T) tileCentroids <- SpatialPointsDataFrame(tileCentroids, data.frame(id = row.names(tileCentroids),stringsAsFactors = F)) distance <- rgeos::gDistance(tileCentroids, pts, byid=T) tile_pref <- t(apply(distance,1, function(x) rank(x,ties.method ="random"))) solved <- solve_LSAP(tile_pref, maximum = FALSE) solved_cols <- as.numeric(solved) newDat <- data.frame(tile_region= row.names(tile_pref), id = as.numeric(colnames(tile_pref)[solved_cols]), stringsAsFactors = F) newTiles <- tiles newTiles@data <- join(newTiles@data, newDat, by="id") newTiles <- newTiles[!is.na(newTiles$tile_region),] return(newTiles) } Make a tilegram of africa. tileGram <- makeTilegram(afr) tileGram_f <- fortify(tileGram) tileGram_f <- join(tileGram_f, tileGram@data,by="id") p <- ggplot(tileGram_f) + geom_polygon(aes(long, lat, group = group, fill=as.numeric(id), text = tile_region)) +coord_equal() + theme(legend.position = "none", panel.background = element_blank(), axis.title = element_blank(), axis.text = element_blank(), axis.ticks = element_blank()) ggplotly(width = 500,height=500) {"x":{"data":[{"x":[2967590.93893105,3391532.50163548,3815474.06433992,3815474.06433992,3391532.50163548,2967590.93893105,2967590.93893105,2967590.93893105],"y":[-3671441.63022115,-3426678.85487307,-3671441.63022115,-4160967.1809173,-4405729.95626538,-4160967.1809173,-3671441.63022115,-3671441.63022115],"text":"as.numeric(id): 7
